intro_band.agent_remit_summary: Aider is an interactive terminal AI pair programmer that runs on the developer's own machine and authority against a single local git repository. It reads repository files the developer shares into the chat, proposes and applies edits to accomplish natural-language requests, auto-commits changes with descriptive messages, and can lint edited files and run developer-authorized tests. Its authorized counterparties are narrow: the developer (trusted operational direction), the configured LLM provider, the developer's git remote, developer-supplied scrape URLs, and optional opt-in analytics and PyPI version checks. The remit forbids autonomous git push, out-of-repository writes, unconfirmed shell execution, package installs without approval, sending code or secrets to any telemetry or third-party endpoint, and following instructions embedded in untrusted file, comment, or web content.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python CLI application; the aider/coders/ package holds a Coder class hierarchy (rooted in base_coder.py) that constructs prompts, applies edit blocks, and orchestrates the edit-lint-commit loop, with per-format prompt modules. Shell execution runs through run_cmd.py (subprocess.Popen(..., shell=True) or a pexpect interactive shell); LLM-suggested shell commands are gated in handle_shell_commands() by an explicit-yes confirmation. File writes resolve through abs_root_path() / utils.safe_abs_path(), which normalize the path but do not confine it to the repository root. External content enters the model context from repo files, the repo map, git history, web scrapes (commands.py cmd_web, scrape.py), and AI!/AI? watch comments (watch.py). Telemetry goes to PostHog (analytics.py) with hardcoded ingestion keys and exception autocapture enabled; dependencies are pinned in requirements.txt. No autonomous git-push path exists anywhere in the package.

behavior_summary: Aider's dominant security posture is a genuine, well-implemented human-in-the-loop confirmation model layered over a deliberately broad code-editing and shell-suggesting capability. The load-bearing control is the explicit-yes gate on LLM-suggested shell commands (io.confirm_ask with explicit_yes_required, which even --yes-always does not bypass) — this is what breaks the otherwise-classic external-content-to-shell-execution chain, so no finding escalates to Critical. The recurring gap is that the remit's hardest boundaries are enforced by confirmation prompts and prompt text rather than deterministic code: there is no repository-root write confinement (safe_abs_path resolves .. and symlinks without containment), no secret scanning before content reaches the LLM or a commit, no elevated approval for editing security-sensitive files, and confirm-bypass modes plus a silent pandoc network download widen the surface further.

rules.rule_text: Act as an interactive AI pair programmer in the developer's terminal: read source code the developer shares into the chat, propose edits to accomplish the developer's natural-language requests, apply approved edits to the local working tree, and commit them to git with a message describing the change — always leaving the developer in control and able to review or undo any change.
Deploying code, publishing packages, or running CI/CD pipelines.
Running as a background service, daemon, scheduled job, or cron task — Aider is interactive and developer-driven.
Aider must never push autonomously.
Anonymous, aggregate usage metrics only; must never carry code, prompts, keys, or PII.
Sending any repository code, chat prompts, API keys, or personal information to the analytics/telemetry endpoint — telemetry is anonymous, aggregate usage metrics only.
Writing, staging, or committing any secret-like string into git history or a commit message.
Including secret-like strings from the repository or `.env` in LLM context, proposed edits, or commit messages.
Reading files excluded by the project's ignore rules (`.gitignore`, `.aiderignore`) unless the developer has explicitly added the file to the chat.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Executing any shell command, including `/run`, `/test`, and any lint/test command that runs code — the developer must see and confirm the exact command, and any LLM-*proposed* command must be shown verbatim before it runs.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Editing security-sensitive files: container build files (Dockerfiles), CI/CD workflow definitions, dependency manifests and lockfiles, code-ownership files (CODEOWNERS), and security-policy files.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Adding to the chat any file that is not already tracked in the repository; a request to add a file from **outside** the repository as an editable target must be refused, not merely confirmed.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Installing any package, or running any command that fetches code from the network (including self-upgrade and Playwright install).
Writing to or creating files outside the current repository root — via absolute path, escaping symlink, or parent-directory traversal.
Pushing commits to a remote without an explicit developer command.
Executing a shell command the developer did not explicitly author or confirm, including any command the LLM proposed that was not shown verbatim for approval.

Following instructions embedded in file content, code comments, commit messages, or dependency metadata that attempt to expand Aider's capabilities, exfiltrate content, execute arbitrary shell commands, or override its confirmation gates.
Installing packages, or modifying the developer's shell environment, OS packages, or system state.
Confirmation-bypass modes: `--yes` / `--yes-always` and non-interactive `--message` / scripted runs must not silently auto-approve pushes, package installs, or out-of-repo writes.

rules.section: Escalation Rules — Halt Agent and Alert Operator
rules.rule_text: A secret-like string about to be written into a commit, edit, or git history.

findings.summary: File edits have no repository-root confinement — abs_root_path/safe_abs_path resolve .. and symlinks but never check the result stays inside the repo.
findings.description: The remit lists writing outside the repository root via absolute path, symlink, or traversal as Never Allowed, but no code enforces containment — safe_abs_path calls Path(res).resolve() and returns it unchecked. An out-of-repo path is stopped only by the generic "Create new file?" / "Allow edits to file not added to the chat?" confirmation, which auto-approves under --yes. The deterministic boundary the remit requires is absent.
policy_rule_text: Writing to or creating files outside the current repository root — via absolute path, escaping symlink, or parent-directory traversal.
Adding to the chat any file that is not already tracked in the repository; a request to add a file from **outside** the repository as an editable target must be refused, not merely confirmed.

evidence.snippet: safe_abs_path does Path(res).resolve() and returns it — resolves .. and symlinks with no check that the result is under the repo root.
allowed_to_edit gates an unknown path only with a generic confirm_ask (non-explicit, so --yes returns yes); abs_root_path (line 566) builds Path(root)/path with no containment.

recommended_actions: In abs_root_path (base_coder.py:566), after resolving, verify the result is within self.root (e.g. os.path.commonpath) and refuse the edit outright when it is not, rather than delegating to a confirm prompt.
Make the out-of-repo case a hard refusal that --yes/--yes-always cannot bypass, matching the remit's Never-Allowed classification.

findings.summary: Untrusted external content enters the LLM context unlabeled — scraped web pages, file contents, git history, and AI!/AI? watch comments carry no origin marking or injection neutralization.
findings.description: The remit forbids following instructions embedded in file content, comments, commit messages, or web pages, but Aider concatenates all of these into the model context with no separation between trusted developer direction and untrusted content. Scraped pages are prefixed only with "Here is the content of {url}:" and pushed into cur_messages; watch-mode feeds whole files surrounding an AI! comment into the prompt. The indirect-injection surface is real; its impact is bounded because downstream edits and shell commands still hit confirmation gates (see positives).
policy_rule_text: Following instructions embedded in file content, code comments, commit messages, or dependency metadata that attempt to expand Aider's capabilities, exfiltrate content, execute arbitrary shell commands, or override its confirmation gates.

evidence.snippet: cmd_web wraps scraped content as "Here is the content of {url}:" and appends it to cur_messages as user content with no sanitization or untrusted-content labeling.
process_changes builds the prompt from whole watched files (TreeContext) around AI!/AI? comments; surrounding file content is untrusted but enters context unmarked.

recommended_actions: Wrap externally-sourced content (web scrapes, watched file bodies, git history) in explicit untrusted-content delimiters in prompt construction so the model is instructed not to treat it as directives.
Keep the explicit-confirmation gates on shell execution and edits as the enforced backstop, and document that they are load-bearing against indirect injection.

findings.summary: No secret scanning before repository content reaches the LLM provider or a commit — the only redaction (scrub_sensitive_info) covers two providers' keys in the settings echo, not content or commits.
findings.description: The remit forbids sending secret-like strings from the repo or .env into LLM context, edits, or commit messages, and lists a secret about to be committed as a halt condition. No detection runs on the content path or in repo.commit; scrub_sensitive_info masks only openai_api_key and anthropic_api_key, and only in the printed settings and command line. A secret in an added file, the repo map, or an auto-commit is neither redacted nor blocked.
policy_rule_text: Writing, staging, or committing any secret-like string into git history or a commit message.
Including secret-like strings from the repository or `.env` in LLM context, proposed edits, or commit messages.
A secret-like string about to be written into a commit, edit, or git history.

evidence.snippet: scrub_sensitive_info only replaces args.openai_api_key and args.anthropic_api_key, and is called only on the settings/command-line echo (main.py:750) — not on content sent to the model or committed.
auto_commit / the content path apply no secret pattern scan before sending file content to the provider or writing a commit.

recommended_actions: Add a secret-pattern scan on file content before it enters the model context and before auto_commit, prompting the developer (or halting) when a high-entropy or known-key pattern is detected.
Broaden redaction beyond the two hardcoded provider keys to a provider-agnostic secret detector shared by the context path and commit path.

findings.summary: Security-sensitive files (Dockerfiles, CI/CD workflows, lockfiles, CODEOWNERS, SECURITY) get no elevated approval — they are edited through the same generic gate as any file.
findings.description: The remit requires approval specifically before editing security-sensitive files, but allowed_to_edit treats every path identically; once a file is in the chat it is edited with no extra confirmation. Aider does maintain a list of important files in special.py, but filter_important_files is wired only into repomap.py to reprioritize the repo map — it never influences edit approval.
policy_rule_text: Editing security-sensitive files: container build files (Dockerfiles), CI/CD workflow definitions, dependency manifests and lockfiles, code-ownership files (CODEOWNERS), and security-policy files.

evidence.snippet: filter_important_files (ROOT_IMPORTANT_FILES includes Dockerfile, CODEOWNERS, lockfiles, SECURITY) is imported only by repomap.py:657 to rank the repo map — not by any edit-approval path.
allowed_to_edit has no branch for sensitive files; an already-added Dockerfile or CI workflow is edited with no confirmation beyond initial add.

recommended_actions: Reuse the special.py important-files classifier in allowed_to_edit to require an explicit confirmation before editing Dockerfiles, CI/CD workflows, dependency manifests/lockfiles, CODEOWNERS, and security-policy files.

findings.summary: Confirm-bypass modes (--yes/--yes-always, scripted runs) auto-approve out-of-repo file creation/edits and untrusted URL scraping, though they preserve the explicit shell-command gate.
findings.description: The remit says confirmation-bypass modes must not silently auto-approve out-of-repo writes. Under self.yes is True, confirm_ask returns yes for every non-explicit prompt — including "Create new file?", "Allow edits to file not added to the chat?", and "Add URL to the chat?" — so file writes (including the unconfined paths of finding 001) and web scraping proceed with no review. The scripted return_coder path forces yes_always=True. Notably the shell gate survives, since it uses explicit_yes_required and returns "n" here.
policy_rule_text: Confirmation-bypass modes: `--yes` / `--yes-always` and non-interactive `--message` / scripted runs must not silently auto-approve pushes, package installs, or out-of-repo writes.

evidence.snippet: "if self.yes is True: res = 'n' if explicit_yes_required else 'y'" — every non-explicit confirm (new file, edit-unadded, add-URL) auto-approves under --yes.
"if return_coder and args.yes_always is None: args.yes_always = True" — scripted/embedded use silently enables the bypass.

recommended_actions: Exclude out-of-repo writes and untrusted-URL scraping from --yes/--yes-always auto-approval (treat them like the shell gate's explicit_yes_required), so the bypass covers only in-repo, low-risk confirmations.

findings.summary: try_pandoc silently downloads a pandoc binary over the network with no developer confirmation and no integrity check, during web-scrape HTML conversion.
findings.description: The remit requires approval before installing packages or running any command that fetches code from the network. While the Playwright install path is confirm-gated, Scraper.try_pandoc calls pypandoc.download_pandoc(delete_installer=True) automatically the first time HTML is converted, fetching and installing an executable with no prompt and no hash/signature verification.
policy_rule_text: Installing any package, or running any command that fetches code from the network (including self-upgrade and Playwright install).
Installing packages, or modifying the developer's shell environment, OS packages, or system state.

evidence.snippet: try_pandoc calls pypandoc.download_pandoc(delete_installer=True) with no io.confirm_ask and no integrity verification, unlike install_playwright which prompts.

recommended_actions: Gate try_pandoc's download_pandoc behind an io.confirm_ask (as install_playwright already does) and verify the downloaded binary's integrity before use, or fall back to raw HTML when pandoc is absent.

findings.summary: PostHog analytics is initialized with enable_exception_autocapture=True, which can transmit stack traces containing file paths and code fragments — beyond the remit's "anonymous, aggregate metrics only".
findings.description: The remit states telemetry must never carry code, prompts, keys, or PII and must be anonymous aggregate usage metrics only. The explicit event() payloads honor this (redacted model names, coarse system info), but enable_exception_autocapture=True ships uncaught-exception stack traces to PostHog, which routinely include local file paths and source-line context. This is opt-in, which bounds the exposure, but the captured content exceeds the declared telemetry scope.
policy_rule_text: Anonymous, aggregate usage metrics only; must never carry code, prompts, keys, or PII.
Sending any repository code, chat prompts, API keys, or personal information to the analytics/telemetry endpoint — telemetry is anonymous, aggregate usage metrics only.

evidence.snippet: Posthog(..., enable_exception_autocapture=True, ...) — autocaptured exception stack traces can carry file paths and code context, unlike the curated event() properties.

recommended_actions: Disable enable_exception_autocapture (or scrub paths/frames before send) so analytics carries only the curated, anonymous event() properties the remit authorizes.

findings.summary: PostHog and Mixpanel project ingestion keys are hardcoded as string literals in analytics.py — low sensitivity (public write-only keys) but a hardcoded-credential hygiene note.
findings.description: analytics.py embeds the PostHog project API key and Mixpanel project token as literals. These are client-side, write-only ingestion identifiers distributed by design in an open-source client, so they do not grant read access to collected data — the sensitivity is low — but hardcoding them centralizes trust in a value that cannot be rotated without a release and is worth surfacing for completeness.

evidence.snippet: mixpanel_project_token and posthog_project_api_key are assigned as literal strings (values redacted here) — public write-only ingestion keys embedded in source.

recommended_actions: Optionally source the telemetry ingestion keys from a build-time constant or config so they can be rotated without a code change; no urgent action given their write-only, public nature.

positives.title: Explicit-yes human-in-the-loop gate on LLM-suggested shell commands
positives.description: LLM-proposed shell commands are shown verbatim and require an explicit yes (explicit_yes_required), which even --yes-always does not auto-approve — returning "n" instead — so the external-content-to-shell-execution chain requires a fresh human confirmation each time.

positives.title: No autonomous git push
positives.description: No push implementation exists anywhere in the agent package; pushes occur only when the developer explicitly types a raw git command via /git, satisfying the remit's prohibition on autonomous pushes.

positives.title: Pinned dependencies with constraints file
positives.description: requirements.txt pins every dependency with == (uv-compiled against requirements/common-constraints.txt) and pyproject.toml sources dependencies from it, limiting version-swap and dependency-confusion exposure.

positives.title: Transcript and history files auto-added to .gitignore
positives.description: check_gitignore offers to add the .aider* patterns (chat/LLM/input history) to .gitignore, helping keep code transcripts and any captured secrets out of version control.

positives.title: Anonymized, opt-in analytics with model-name redaction
positives.description: Analytics is opt-in (asked_opt_in plus a 10% sampling gate), transmits only coarse system info, and redacts unknown model provider names via _redact_model_name.

positives.title: API-key redaction in settings and command-line echo
positives.description: scrub_sensitive_info masks OpenAI and Anthropic API keys in the printed settings and reconstructed command line, reducing accidental key disclosure in that output surface.

rows.purpose: Running record of the chat conversation (user prompts and assistant responses)
Raw LLM request/response content when the developer enables --llm-history-file
Developer's typed input/command history for recall
Structured analytics events when an analytics logfile is configured

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. Aider pairs a real, operative human-in-the-loop control model with a capability surface (arbitrary in-repo edits, shell-command suggestion, web scraping) that is only as strong as its confirmation prompts. Its strongest categories are supply chain (pinned dependencies) and its shell-execution gate; its weakest are the absence of deterministic path confinement and output/secret filtering on the zero-trust axis, and the near-total absence of adversarial-testing evidence within the scanned code. The posture is that of a mature, security-aware developer tool whose declared "never allowed" boundaries lack code-level enforcement to back the prompts that currently guard them.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Aider is structurally scoped to one repository and one interactive session with a fixed tool inventory that matches the remit's Known Good Baseline (no daemon, no cross-repo authority, watch mode confined to the repo root in watch.py filter_func); the main domain gap is the absence of repository-root write confinement in abs_root_path/safe_abs_path.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: External and untrusted content — repo files, repo map, git history, scraped web pages (cmd_web), and AI!/AI? watch comments — enters the LLM context with no origin labeling or injection neutralization, and no secret scanning runs before content is sent; .env is gitignored and the developer chooses what to add, which keeps it above Absent.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: A real human-in-the-loop confirmation model is operative — LLM-suggested shell commands require an explicit yes that --yes-always cannot bypass (io.py 866-867) — but the remit's hard boundaries rely on prompts rather than code: no path confinement, no per-command exec policy, no output/secret filtering, no sensitive-file approval, and confirm-bypass modes auto-approve out-of-repo writes and scraping.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Dependencies are fully pinned (== in requirements.txt via uv compile against common-constraints.txt) and the Playwright install is confirm-gated, which is a real supply-chain control; it is held off higher by the silent unauthenticated pandoc binary download (try_pandoc) and hardcoded telemetry ingestion keys.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No adversarial-testing, injection-test, or red-team artifact is present within the scanned scope (aider/*.py + aider/coders/); Aider's broader repository maintains a test suite outside this scope, so the score reflects the scanned artifacts rather than a claim that the project is untested.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Durable surfaces exist — chat/LLM/input transcript history files and structured (but coarse, ~5 event types) opt-in PostHog analytics — but there is no structured, action-level audit log recording edit, commit, and shell-execution decisions that would support automated detection.
